title: Living Room Inspiration
excerpt: A living room brings together more furniture and daily use than any other room. A simple guide to thinking through each part — sofa, lighting, surfaces, and textiles.

content.text: A living room brings together more furniture and more daily use than any other space in a home, so it rewards a little planning. A good approach is to start with the largest pieces and work down to the details — once the big things are in place, everything else tends to fall into line. Below is a simple way to think about each part. Tap a category to jump straight to the pieces.

The sofa is usually the largest and most-used piece in the room, so it is worth choosing with care. Think about the size of your space, how many people you need to seat, and how the sofa will be used from day to day. Comfortable, hard-wearing upholstery and a shape that suits the room will serve you far longer than any passing trend.

A single overhead light rarely does a living room justice. A mix of sources — a floor lamp, a table lamp, and a little accent light — lets you set the mood for reading, relaxing, or having people over. As a rule, warm-toned bulbs feel more inviting in the evening than cool, bright white ones.

A coffee table, along with one or two side tables, gives everyone somewhere to set things down. Choose surfaces that suit how you actually live — sturdy and easy to clean if the room sees a lot of use — and try to leave a little open space rather than filling every inch.

Rugs, cushions, and throws are among the easiest ways to add warmth, colour, and texture to a room. A rug can help define the seating area, while cushions and throws soften the space and pull your colours together. Layering a few different textures usually feels richer than matching everything exactly.
